expanded_description: Subordinating conjunctions are the words that create dependent clauses, establishing logical relationships between ideas while also determining the grammatical structure of sentences. On the SAT, these words—'because,' 'although,' 'while,' 'if,' 'when,' 'since,' 'unless,' 'after,' 'before,' 'until,' 'as,' 'whereas,' 'even though,' and others—are tested in multiple ways: in sentence boundary questions where they distinguish independent from dependent clauses, in transition questions where they signal logical relationships, and in punctuation questions where their presence or absence determines whether commas and other marks are required. Mastering subordinating conjunctions requires understanding both their semantic function (what logical relationship each signals) and their syntactic function (how each transforms an independent clause into a dependent one, with consequences for the punctuation and structure of the entire sentence).

core_concept.principle:
A subordinating conjunction is a word that introduces a dependent clause, making it grammatically incapable of standing alone as a sentence. The subordinating conjunction achieves two things simultaneously: it signals a specific logical relationship between the dependent clause and the main clause, and it subordinates the clause grammatically so that it must be attached to an independent clause. The resulting structure is a complex sentence, in which the main clause carries the primary assertion and the subordinate clause provides context, cause, condition, concession, time, or contrast.

The analysis of subordinating conjunctions on the SAT requires attention to three dimensions:

(1) Logical Meaning: Each subordinating conjunction signals a specific type of relationship. 'Because' and 'since' signal cause or reason. 'Although,' 'though,' and 'even though' signal concession—an acknowledgment that opposes or limits the main clause. 'While' and 'whereas' signal contrast. 'If,' 'unless,' and 'provided that' signal condition. 'When,' 'after,' 'before,' 'until,' and 'as' signal time or sequence. Using the wrong subordinating conjunction misrepresents the logical relationship between the ideas.

(2) Grammatical Subordination: The presence of a subordinating conjunction makes a clause dependent. A group of words that would be an independent clause—'the experiment succeeded'—becomes dependent when a subordinating conjunction is added: 'Because the experiment succeeded...' The dependent clause cannot stand alone as a sentence; it must be attached to a main clause. Recognizing this grammatical transformation is essential for sentence boundary questions.

(3) Punctuation Rules: When a dependent clause introduced by a subordinating conjunction precedes the main clause, it is followed by a comma. When the dependent clause follows the main clause, no comma is typically needed (unless the clause is nonrestrictive). These punctuation rules are tested on the SAT. 'Because the experiment succeeded, the researchers celebrated.' (Comma required after introductory dependent clause.) 'The researchers celebrated because the experiment succeeded.' (No comma before dependent clause following the main clause.)
core_concept.explanation:
Consider the independent clause 'The experiment succeeded.' It can stand alone as a complete sentence. Now add a subordinating conjunction: 'Because the experiment succeeded.' The clause is now dependent—it creates an expectation of a main clause to complete the thought. 'Because the experiment succeeded, what?' The subordinating conjunction has transformed a complete thought into an incomplete one, demanding a main clause to resolve the logical and grammatical expectation it creates. 'Because the experiment succeeded, the researchers celebrated.' The complex sentence is now complete: the subordinate clause provides the cause, and the main clause provides the effect.

The SAT frequently tests subordinating conjunctions in the context of sentence boundary errors. A common error is punctuating a dependent clause as if it were a sentence: 'Because the experiment succeeded.' This is a fragment—the subordinating conjunction has made the clause dependent, and it cannot stand alone. The correction is either to attach it to a main clause or to remove the subordinating conjunction: 'The experiment succeeded.' Another common error is using a subordinating conjunction to connect clauses but punctuating incorrectly, either by omitting the required comma after an introductory dependent clause or by inserting a comma before a dependent clause that follows the main clause when no comma is needed.

Transition questions on the SAT also test subordinating conjunctions alongside coordinating conjunctions and conjunctive adverbs. The student must select the word that both correctly signals the logical relationship and fits the grammatical structure of the sentence. A blank may require a subordinating conjunction because the clause it introduces is dependent, ruling out coordinating conjunctions or conjunctive adverbs that would require a different grammatical structure.

image.alt: A diagram with a central box labeled 'Subordinating Conjunctions' surrounded by boxes representing logical relationships: Cause (because, since), Concession (although, though, even though), Contrast (while, whereas), Condition (if, unless, provided that), Time (when, after, before, until, as). Each box contains example sentences showing the subordinating conjunction in use. A separate section shows punctuation rules: comma after introductory dependent clause, no comma when dependent clause follows main clause.
image.caption: Subordinating conjunctions signal logical relationships while transforming independent clauses into dependent ones, with specific punctuation rules governing the resulting complex sentences

sections.section_title: Foundation: Logical Relationships and Grammatical Rules
sections.introduction: Before students can answer questions involving subordinating conjunctions on the SAT, they must internalize the specific logical relationships each conjunction signals and the grammatical and punctuation rules that govern complex sentences. This section catalogs the major subordinating conjunctions by logical category and defines the rules for punctuating sentences that contain them.

key_concept.definition:
Subordinating conjunctions are the words that create dependent clauses and, in doing so, signal specific logical relationships between the dependent clause and the main clause. The choice of conjunction determines how the reader understands the connection between the ideas. Using the wrong conjunction creates a logical mismatch, which the SAT tests in transition questions. Using a conjunction correctly but punctuating the resulting complex sentence incorrectly creates a punctuation error, which the SAT tests in sentence boundary and punctuation questions.

The major subordinating conjunctions, organized by logical relationship:

Cause/Reason: because, since, as, now that. These introduce the reason or cause for the main clause's assertion. 'Because the funding was cut, the project was cancelled.'

Concession: although, though, even though, while (when meaning 'although'). These acknowledge a point that seems to oppose the main clause but does not defeat it. 'Although the funding was cut, the project continued on a reduced scale.'

Contrast: whereas, while (when meaning 'in contrast'). These juxtapose two differing or opposing ideas. 'Whereas the first experiment succeeded, the second failed.'

Condition: if, unless, provided that, as long as, in case. These establish a condition under which the main clause holds. 'If the funding is approved, the project will begin in June.'

Time/Sequence: when, after, before, until, while (when meaning 'during the time that'), as, once, since (when temporal). These establish temporal relationships. 'After the funding was approved, the project began immediately.'

Purpose: so that, in order that. These establish the purpose or goal of the main clause's action. 'The researchers published their data so that other teams could verify the results.'
key_concept.context: SAT questions involving subordinating conjunctions appear in multiple formats: transition questions requiring the correct logical connector, sentence boundary questions testing for fragments, and punctuation questions testing comma usage in complex sentences.

categories.description: The comma rules that govern sentences with subordinating conjunctions.

examples.explanation:
When a dependent clause introduced by a subordinating conjunction comes before the main clause, it is an introductory element and must be followed by a comma. The comma marks the boundary between the introductory material and the main clause, guiding the reader to the grammatical core of the sentence.

Correct: 'Because the storm knocked out power lines, the city was without electricity for three days.' (Comma after introductory dependent clause.)
Correct: 'Although the sample size was small, the results were statistically significant.' (Comma after introductory dependent clause.)
Correct: 'When the experiment concluded, the researchers analyzed the data.' (Comma after introductory dependent clause.)

Error (missing comma): 'Because the storm knocked out power lines the city was without electricity for three days.' The absence of the comma makes the sentence harder to parse and, by SAT standards, is a punctuation error.

examples.process: Dependent Clauses Following the Main Clause
examples.explanation:
When the dependent clause follows the main clause, no comma is typically needed. The dependent clause flows naturally from the main clause without a punctuation boundary. The exception is when the dependent clause is nonrestrictive—providing additional, nonessential information—in which case a comma may be used, but this is rare with subordinating conjunctions and more common with relative clauses.

Correct: 'The city was without electricity for three days because the storm knocked out power lines.' (No comma before the dependent clause.)
Correct: 'The results were statistically significant although the sample size was small.' (No comma before the dependent clause.)
Correct: 'The researchers analyzed the data when the experiment concluded.' (No comma before the dependent clause.)

Error (unnecessary comma): 'The city was without electricity for three days, because the storm knocked out power lines.' The comma before 'because' is unnecessary and by many standards incorrect when the dependent clause is restrictive (essential to the meaning).

worked_examples.title: Selecting and Punctuating Subordinating Conjunctions
worked_examples.problem:
For each pair of ideas, select the correct subordinating conjunction and correctly punctuate the resulting complex sentence.

Pair A: The team trained extensively. They lost the championship game. (Concessive relationship.)

Pair B: The museum closed for renovations. It will reopen next spring. (Temporal relationship.)

step_by_step.explanation: Logical relationship: The training would normally lead to winning, but the second idea presents the opposite outcome. This is concession—the main point (losing) is true despite the expected cause (training). Appropriate subordinating conjunctions: 'although,' 'though,' 'even though.' Using 'although' to introduce the dependent clause: 'Although the team trained extensively, they lost the championship game.' The dependent clause comes first, so a comma is required after it. Alternative (main clause first): 'The team lost the championship game although they trained extensively.' No comma before the dependent clause.

Logical relationship: The closing happens first; the reopening happens later. This is a temporal sequence. Appropriate subordinating conjunctions: 'after,' 'when,' 'once.' Using 'after' to introduce the dependent clause: 'After the museum closed for renovations, it will reopen next spring.' The dependent clause comes first, so a comma is required. Alternative (main clause first): 'The museum will reopen next spring after it closes for renovations.' No comma before the dependent clause. Note: Verb tense must be adjusted for temporal logic—'closed' (past) and 'will reopen' (future) create a slight tension if used with 'after' in the same sentence; 'After the museum closes for renovations, it will reopen next spring' uses present tense in the dependent clause for future meaning, which is standard English usage.

worked_examples.final_answer: Pair A: 'Although the team trained extensively, they lost the championship game.' Concessive subordinating conjunction with comma after introductory dependent clause. Pair B: 'After the museum closes for renovations, it will reopen next spring.' Temporal subordinating conjunction with comma after introductory dependent clause. Both demonstrate the correct use of subordinating conjunctions to establish logical relationships and the correct punctuation of the resulting complex sentences.
worked_examples.concept_applied: This example demonstrates the dual nature of subordinating conjunction use: selecting the conjunction that correctly signals the logical relationship, and applying the correct punctuation rules to the complex sentence.

image.alt: A table with rows for each logical category (Cause, Concession, Contrast, Condition, Time, Purpose). Each row lists the common conjunctions, provides a correctly punctuated example, and notes the logical relationship signaled.
image.caption: Subordinating conjunctions by logical category: each signals a specific relationship and governs the punctuation of the resulting complex sentence

sections.introduction: This section provides practice with the specific formats of SAT questions that test subordinating conjunctions. Passages with blanks or underlined portions are accompanied by questions that require selecting the correct conjunction and applying the appropriate punctuation.

key_concept.definition: SAT questions involving subordinating conjunctions test three skills: selecting the conjunction that correctly signals the logical relationship, recognizing and correcting sentence boundary errors (fragments) caused by misused subordinating conjunctions, and applying correct punctuation to complex sentences. The strategic approach: (1) Diagnose the logical relationship between the clauses. (2) Determine whether the blank or underlined portion requires a subordinating conjunction (creating a complex sentence) or a different grammatical structure. (3) If a subordinating conjunction is required, select the one that matches the logical relationship. (4) Apply the correct punctuation based on the order of clauses.
key_concept.context: Subordinating conjunction questions are integrated across the SAT Writing and Language section and also appear in transition questions on the Reading section.

categories.type: Practice: SAT-Style Conjunction and Punctuation Questions
categories.description: Passage excerpts requiring the correct subordinating conjunction and punctuation.

examples.explanation:
Passage: '______ the archaeological site had been extensively studied for decades, the recent discovery of a hidden chamber suggested that much about the ancient civilization remained unknown.'

Which choice best completes the sentence?
A) Because
B) Although
C) After
D) If

Analysis: The first clause describes extensive prior study (which would normally lead to complete knowledge). The main clause describes a discovery of something unknown (suggesting knowledge was incomplete). The relationship is concessive: despite extensive study, something remained unknown. 'Although' (Choice B) correctly signals concession. 'Because' (Choice A) would incorrectly signal that the extensive study caused the discovery of a hidden chamber. 'After' (Choice C) would signal temporal sequence, which is not the primary relationship. 'If' (Choice D) would signal condition, which does not fit the factual context. The sentence also requires a comma after the introductory dependent clause, which is correctly present in the original.

image.alt: An annotated SAT-style question with the logical relationship diagnosed, the correct subordinating conjunction explained, and the punctuation rule noted.
image.caption: SAT subordinating conjunction questions require matching the conjunction to the logical relationship and applying correct punctuation

summary:
Subordinating conjunctions are the words that create dependent clauses, establishing logical relationships while also determining the grammatical structure of sentences. They serve a dual function: each conjunction signals a specific logical relationship (cause, concession, contrast, condition, time, purpose), and each transforms an independent clause into a dependent one that must be attached to a main clause. Mastery of subordinating conjunctions requires understanding both dimensions and applying them correctly in context.

The logical dimension: 'Because' signals cause; 'although' signals concession; 'whereas' signals contrast; 'if' signals condition; 'when' and 'after' signal time; 'so that' signals purpose. Selecting the correct conjunction requires accurately diagnosing the logical relationship between the ideas. The SAT tests this in transition questions, where the student must choose among subordinating conjunctions, coordinating conjunctions, and conjunctive adverbs. The correct choice signals the right relationship and fits the grammatical structure of the sentence.

The grammatical dimension: A subordinating conjunction makes a clause dependent. An independent clause that could stand alone as a sentence ('the experiment succeeded') becomes dependent and grammatically incomplete when a subordinating conjunction is added ('Because the experiment succeeded...'). Punctuating a dependent clause as a sentence creates a fragment, which the SAT tests in sentence boundary questions. The correction is either to attach the dependent clause to an independent clause or to remove the subordinating conjunction.

The punctuation dimension: When a dependent clause introduced by a subordinating conjunction precedes the main clause, it must be followed by a comma. When it follows the main clause, no comma is typically needed. These rules are absolute in formal writing and are tested on the SAT. The comma after an introductory dependent clause is not optional; it is required to mark the boundary between the subordinate and main elements of the sentence. Mastery of subordinating conjunctions is thus mastery of a cluster of related skills: logical analysis, grammatical analysis, and punctuation precision. Together, these skills enable the sophisticated sentence construction that characterizes mature academic writing.
